request.question: What is the name of the scientific journal established by the Indian Physicist who earned the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics

title: Physical Review
Zeitschrift für Geologische Wissenschaften
K. S. Krishnan
Indian Journal of Physics
Journal of Physics: Condensed Matter
Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres
C. V. Raman
Journal of the Chemical Society
Canadian Journal of Earth Sciences
Canadian Journal of Physiology and Pharmacology
sentences: Physical Review is an American peer-reviewed scientific journal established in 1893 by Edward Nichols.
It publishes original research as well as scientific and literature reviews on all aspects of physics.
It is published by the American Physical Society (APS).
The journal is in its third series, and is split in several sub-journals each covering a particular field of physics.
It has a sister journal, "Physical Review Letters", which publishes shorter articles of broader interest.

The Zeitschrift für Geologische Wissenschaften (English: Journal for the Geological Sciences ) is a peer-reviewed German scientific journal established in 1973, that publishes preferably original papers in German and English dealing with geology and related sub-disciplines.
Since 2005 the journal has been published bimonthly by the Verlag für Geowissenschaften Berlin.

The journal was formerly an official periodical of the national East German Gesellschaft für Geologische Wissenschaften, the East German geological sciences society, and was published by the scientific and academic publishing house Akademie Verlag.
The journal was regarded as the most important geological sciences periodical in East Germany with an international reputation.
The journal is indexed in: GeoRef, Chemical Abstracts Service, and Geoline.
Sir Kariamanickam Srinivasa Krishnan, FRS, (4 December 1898 – 14 June 1961) was an Indian physicist.
He was a co-discoverer of Raman scattering, for which his mentor C. V. Raman was awarded the 1930 Nobel Prize in Physics.
The Indian Journal of Physics is a monthly peer-reviewed scientific journal published by Springer Science+Business Media on behalf of the Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science.
It was established in 1926 by C. V. Raman and covers applied, experimental, and theoretical physics.
The editor-in-chief is Subham Majumdar.
Journal of Physics: Condensed Matter is a weekly peer-reviewed scientific journal established in 1989 and published by IOP Publishing.
The journal covers all areas of condensed matter physics including soft matter and nanostructures.
The editor-in-chief is Jason Gardner (National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center and Australian Nuclear Science and Technology Organisation).
Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres is a peer-reviewed scientific journal established in 1968 covering astrobiology and origins of life research.
It is the official journal of the International Society for the Study of the Origin of Life.
The journal's scope includes research on the origin, evolution, distribution, and future of life on Earth and beyond.

Some examples of areas of interest are: prebiotic chemistry and the nature of Earth's early environment, self-replicating and self-organizing systems, the RNA world hypothesis and of other possible precursor systems, and the problem of the origin of the genetic code.
According to the "Journal Citation Reports", the journal has a 2016 impact factor of 1.000.
Due to recent progress in paleontological studies it has been possible to increase the age of the earliest organisms from an estimated 2.0–3.0 billion years to 3.5–3.8 billion years.

Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman (7 November 188821 November 1970) was an Indian physicist born in the former Madras Province in India presently called as Tamil Nadu, who carried out ground-breaking work in the field of light scattering, which earned him the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics.
He discovered that when light traverses a transparent material, some of the deflected light changes wavelength.
This phenomenon, subsequently known as Raman scattering, results from the Raman effect.
In 1954, India honoured him with its highest civilian award, the Bharat Ratna.
The Journal of the Chemical Society was a scientific journal established by the Chemical Society in 1849 as the Quarterly Journal of the Chemical Society.
The journal underwent several renamings, splits, and mergers throughout its history.
In 1980, the Chemical Society merged with several other organizations into the Royal Society of Chemistry.
The journal's continuity is found in "Chemical Communications", "Dalton Transactions", "Faraday Transactions", and "Perkin Transactions", all of which are published by the Royal Society of Chemistry.
The Canadian Journal of Earth Sciences is a monthly peer-reviewed scientific journal established in 1963, which reports current research on all aspects of the Earth sciences.
It is published by NRC Research Press.
The journal also publishes special issues that focus on information and studies limited in scope to a specific segment of the Earth sciences.
The editor-in-chief is Ali Polat (University of Windsor).
According to the "Journal Citation Reports", the journal has a 2012 impact factor of 1.366.
The Canadian Journal of Physiology and Pharmacology (fr.

Revue canadienne de physiologie et pharmacologie) is a monthly peer-reviewed scientific journal established in 1964 after the split of "Canadian Journal of Biochemistry and Physiology" in two parts, the other one being "Canadian Journal of Biochemistry".
While the vast majority of its papers are in English, the journal also publishes in French.
Abstracts for its papers are in both languages.
The journal is published monthly by NRC Research Press.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What is the name of the scientific journal established by the Indian Physicist who earned the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics
hop1_docs: Arthur Edward Ruark | Arthur Edward Ruark (1899–1979) was an American physicist who actively played a role in the development of quantum mechanics.  He wrote the book "Atoms, Molecules, and Quanta" with Nobel Prize in Chemistry winner Harold Clayton Urey in 1930, and is the author of numerous scientific papers on quantum physics.

Percy Williams Bridgman | Percy Williams Bridgman (21 April 1882 – 20 August 1961) was an American physicist who won the 1946 Nobel Prize in Physics for his work on the physics of high pressures.  He also wrote extensively on the scientific method and on other aspects of the philosophy of science.  The Bridgman effect is named for him.

Luis Walter Alvarez | Luis Walter Alvarez (June 13, 1911 – September 1, 1988) was an American experimental physicist, inventor, and professor who was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1968.  The American Journal of Physics commented, "Luis Alvarez was one of the most brilliant and productive experimental physicists of the twentieth century."

K. S. Krishnan | Sir Kariamanickam Srinivasa Krishnan, FRS, (4 December 1898 – 14 June 1961) was an Indian physicist.  He was a co-discoverer of Raman scattering, for which his mentor C. V. Raman was awarded the 1930 Nobel Prize in Physics.

C. V. Raman | Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman (7 November 188821 November 1970) was an Indian physicist born in the former Madras Province in India presently called as Tamil Nadu, who carried out ground-breaking work in the field of light scattering, which earned him the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics.  He discovered that when light traverses a transparent material, some of the deflected light changes wavelength.  This phenomenon, subsequently known as Raman scattering, results from the Raman effect.  In 1954, India honoured him with its highest civilian award, the Bharat Ratna.

Indian Physical Society | The Indian Physical Society is a professional society of physicists in India.  It was formally established in 1934 by pioneering Indian physicist Meghnad Saha.  The society's stated objectives are to promote the progress and uphold the cause of both pure and applied physics in India, to encourage publications in physics and related areas, to publisher books, journals, proceedings etc., to organize conferences, advise government bodies, and to secure and administer funds, grants and endowments for the furtherance of scientific research.

Nobel Prize in Physics | The Nobel Prize in Physics (Swedish: "Nobelpriset i fysik" ) is a yearly award given by the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences for those who conferred the most outstanding contributions for mankind in the field of physics.  It is one of the five Nobel Prizes established by the will of Alfred Nobel in 1895 and awarded since 1901; the others being the Nobel Prize in Chemistry, Nobel Prize in Literature, Nobel Peace Prize, and Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.
response.summary_1: Passage [5] identifies the bridge entity: "Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman" (C. V. Raman) and states he "earned the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics." The passages do not state which scientific journal he established. Next-hop retrieval: search for "scientific journal established by C. V. Raman" or "journal founded by Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman" (use biographies of C. V. Raman or lists of journals he founded) to find the journal's name. Use the anchor phrase "Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman ... earned the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics" when retrieving.
response.hop2_query: Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman founded scientific journal
hop2_docs: Raman Science Centre | The Raman Science Centre and Raman Planetarium Complex at Nagpur is an interactive science centre affiliated with Mumbai's Nehru Science Centre.  The centre was developed to promote a scientific attitude, portray the growth of science and technology and their applications in industry and human welfare, and hold science exhibits.  The centre is named after famous Nobel Prize winner Indian physicist Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman.

C. V. Raman | Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman (7 November 188821 November 1970) was an Indian physicist born in the former Madras Province in India presently called as Tamil Nadu, who carried out ground-breaking work in the field of light scattering, which earned him the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics.  He discovered that when light traverses a transparent material, some of the deflected light changes wavelength.  This phenomenon, subsequently known as Raman scattering, results from the Raman effect.  In 1954, India honoured him with its highest civilian award, the Bharat Ratna.

Raman Research Institute | Raman Research Institute (RRI) is an institute of scientific research located in Bangalore, India.  It was founded by Nobel laureate C. V. Raman.  Although it began as an institute privately owned by Sir C. V. Raman, it is now funded by the government of India.

National Science Day | National Science Day is celebrated in India on 28 February each year to mark the discovery of the Raman effect by Indian physicist Sir Chandrashekhara Venkata Raman on 28 February 1928.

Journal of Raman Spectroscopy | The Journal of Raman Spectroscopy is a monthly peer-reviewed scientific journal covering all aspects of Raman spectroscopy, including Higher Order Processes, and Brillouin and Rayleigh scattering.  It was established in 1973 and is published by John Wiley & Sons.  The current editor-in-chief is Laurence A.Nafie (Syracuse University).

Kailasa Venkata Ramiah | Kailasa Venkata Ramiah (20 October 1926 - 24 December 1994) was a renowned educationist from Andhra Pradesh,India.  He was born in Pegallapadu village of Khammam district.  He stood first-in-first in both B.Sc and M.Sc from Osmania University and grew to Head the Physics Department of Osmania University.  He is recipient of Sir Akbar Hyderi Gold medal and several awards during his glittering career both as student and later as an academician.  He finished his post doctral (Phd) in record time and later his research was in Quantum Mechanics and "Raman Effect".  He published about 100 papers in both National and International journals.  He served to become the First Vice-Chancellor of Kakatiya University ( 1976–79).  He founded the University from its inception.It was his vision and efforts that led to the tremendous development of Kakatiya University in a short period of time.

Bangalore Venkata Raman | Bangalore Venkata Raman (8 August 1912 – 20 December 1998) was one of the most important astrologers in modern India and an author of numerous books and articles.  He was instrumental in making Vedic or Hindu astrology known and respected throughout India and the world.  With the help of his sons Niranjan Babu and Sachidananda Babu, he also started the Raman & Rajeswari Research Foundation to promote the knowledge of Astrology and Vastu Shastra.
response.summary_2: Sir Chandrasekhara Venkata Raman (C. V. Raman) earned the 1930 Nobel Prize for Physics. The retrieved passages do not identify any scientific journal he established; they note a "Journal of Raman Spectroscopy" ("established in 1973") but give no link showing Raman founded it.
response.answer: No scientific journal established by C. V. Raman is identified in the summaries.
